results.note: Includes environment-dependent Tirith, POSIX-assumption, encoding and symlink failures.
All nine failures reproduce Windows absolute-path approval bypasses.
Two failures require UTF-8 default encoding and Windows symlink privilege.
Four Windows skill-command regressions and one delegated heartbeat timing failure.

findings.title: Windows absolute paths bypass sensitive-write approval detection
findings.detail: The approval normalizer rewrites resolved home paths only when they begin with '/'. Native Windows paths such as C:\Users\... are not folded to ~/ and redirect, tee, sed, perl and ruby writes toward shell startup files, SSH authorized_keys and Hermes config are not classified dangerous.

findings.title: Hermes is not a suitable SEED safety boundary
findings.detail: Hermes exposes broad shell, autonomous memory, skill mutation, cron and gateway capabilities. Its policy and approval layer cannot substitute for OS-level isolation, especially with the confirmed Windows path bypass.

findings.title: Delegated heartbeat test is timing-fragile
findings.detail: The long-running-tool heartbeat fixture produced six touches where more than six were required, indicating a boundary timing regression or Windows scheduler sensitivity.

findings.title: Hermes skill architecture remains useful as a reference
findings.detail: Registry, tool search, scoped delegation, skill AST audit and most approval tests passed. These patterns may be reimplemented behind SEED governance without importing Hermes runtime.

verdict: Reject as runtime or security boundary on Windows; retain only as a design reference for registry, skills and scoped delegation.
